name: ServiceNow, Inc.

profile.description: ServiceNow is the leading enterprise AI platform, orchestrating 80 billion+ enterprise workflows annually across IT, HR, customer service, security, and finance. The company's Now Platform connects systems, data, and processes with AI to eliminate friction in enterprise operations. ServiceNow serves over 8,100 customers including 85% of the Fortune 500, completed the $7.75B Armis cybersecurity acquisition, and is tracking Now Assist toward $1.5B in 2026 ACV with FY2026 subscription revenue guidance of $15.7–$15.8B (+21% YoY).

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: ServiceNow has fundamentally transformed from an ITSM ticketing system to an enterprise AI platform — a successful business model pivot that expanded TAM from ~$20B to $220B+. Innovation track record (3/3): the platform now orchestrates AI agents across enterprise workflows, and the company was named to Fast Company's Most Innovative Companies 2026 list in Applied AI[1][2]. AI deployment (2/2): Now Assist is tracking toward $1.5B in 2026 ACV (raised from $1B target), with customers spending $1M+ on Now Assist growing 130%+ YoY; the company launched AI Governance features to navigate EU AI Act compliance[2][5]. Market share (1/2): ServiceNow is the category leader in enterprise workflow orchestration with no close independent rival. Pipeline (2/2): the $7.75B Armis acquisition (completed May 2026) more than triples the addressable security and risk TAM, and the new Autonomous Security & Risk product integrating Armis and Veza governs every AI agent, identity, and connected asset[3]. R&D (0/1): sector-average intensity. Total: 8.

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: ServiceNow's mission to 'make the world of work, work better for people' is specific, thoughtful, and connected to a genuine societal benefit — removing friction from the workplace to free humans for higher-value work. Mission floor: 3 (specific and validated by product outcomes). The company runs ServiceNow.org providing platform access to nonprofits, has committed to carbon neutrality, and was named to Ethisphere's 2026 World's Most Ethical Companies list — qualifying for 1 community impact point[5]. CEO Bill McDermott extended his contract to 2030 and leads with a 'people before profits' philosophy, emphasizing that 'great cultures drive great results'[4]. McDermott's prior $3M open-market share purchase demonstrates insider conviction — qualifying for 2 points. Employee satisfaction remains top-tier for enterprise software with CEO approval ratings above 90% on Glassdoor[4]. Total: 3 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 7.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: ServiceNow's financial infrastructure is near-exemplary for a $15B+ revenue growth company. Q1 2026 subscription revenues hit $3,671M (+22% YoY, +19% constant currency), beating the high end of guidance[1]. Full-year 2026 subscription revenue guidance was raised to $15,735–$15,775M (+21% YoY). The company had 16 transactions over $5M in net new ACV in Q1 (+80% YoY), ended with 630 customers above $5M ACV (+22% YoY), and new logo ACV growth accelerated to over 50% YoY[1][2]. The $7.75B Armis acquisition (completed May 2026) extends the platform into cybersecurity, tripling the security TAM[3]. Non-GAAP operating margin of 32% is expanding. Forward P/E is elevated (~22x) and PEG of 2.8 is above the 1.0 target, reflecting premium growth valuation. Liquidity well above $1M floor.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: ServiceNow's core thesis is that AI creates more demand for enterprise workflow orchestration, not less. CEO McDermott has positioned ServiceNow as the 'Operating System for AI,' arguing that 'AI excels at identifying problems and providing advice but cannot execute solutions' — and ServiceNow controls the execution layer[4]. This thesis is being validated by metrics: Now Assist is tracking toward $1.5B in 2026 ACV (raised from $1B), customers spending $1M+ on Now Assist grew 130%+ YoY, and the company was named to Fast Company's Most Innovative Companies 2026 list in Applied AI[2][5]. The $7.75B Armis acquisition extends the AI resilience story: Autonomous Security & Risk integrates Armis and Veza to govern every AI agent, identity, and connected asset — making ServiceNow essential infrastructure for secure AI deployment[3]. The company also launched AI Governance features within the Now Platform to navigate EU AI Act requirements, positioning it as the compliance layer for enterprise AI[5]. Risk protection: 7 (PROTECTED — enterprise workflow execution layer that AI requires, not competes with). AI opportunity: 1 (Now Assist generating quantifiable $1.5B ACV trajectory). Total: 8, reflecting remaining uncertainty about whether agentic AI could eventually replace rather than augment workflow orchestration.

infinite_game.summary: Bill McDermott is a rare enterprise software CEO who operates explicitly with long-term platform thinking. His leadership style — described as 'people before profits,' customer-obsessed, and execution-focused — has steered ServiceNow through a pivotal transformation into what he calls the 'Agentic AI' era[4]. The Board's vision statement — to become 'the Defining AI Enterprise Software Company of the 21st Century' — is an explicitly infinite aspiration. McDermott's contract extension to 2030, his prior $3M share purchase during the sector selloff, and the $7.75B Armis acquisition demonstrate conviction in the long-term platform strategy. ServiceNow's recognition by Ethisphere as one of the World's Most Ethical Companies 2026 validates the organizational culture[5].

just_cause.analysis: ServiceNow's cause — making work work better for people by eliminating enterprise friction — is genuine, service-oriented, and specific. It describes a future state (frictionless enterprise workflows powered by AI) that doesn't fully exist yet and invites both technology partners and customers to contribute. McDermott's consistent articulation of this cause across economic cycles and competitive environments, combined with the Board's framing of ServiceNow as 'the Defining AI Enterprise Software Company of the 21st Century,' earns 2 Just Cause points (genuine, partially validated)[4].

trusting_teams.analysis: ServiceNow's employee culture consistently ranks in the top tier for enterprise software, with CEO approval ratings above 90% on Glassdoor. McDermott emphasizes 'people before profits' and has framed AI as amplifying employees rather than replacing them — creating genuine psychological safety during the AI transition[4]. The company's investment in employee AI training and its 'human-AI' narrative demonstrates institutional commitment. ServiceNow's recognition by Ethisphere as one of the World's Most Ethical Companies 2026 provides external validation of organizational trust[5]. Low voluntary attrition relative to tech sector peers. Score: 2 points (high-trust organization).

worthy_rivals.analysis: McDermott publicly acknowledges Salesforce, SAP, and Microsoft as worthy rivals whose integrations push ServiceNow to develop better connectors and expand platform capabilities. The $7.75B Armis acquisition and prior Moveworks acquisition reflect competitive awareness — rivals' strengths in security (Microsoft Sentinel, Palo Alto) and AI customer service (Salesforce Agentforce) revealing ServiceNow's portfolio gaps[3]. The launch of Autonomous Security & Risk was a direct response to competitive pressure. Score: 1 point.

existential_flexibility.analysis: The pivot from ITSM ticketing (2004–2015) to enterprise platform (2015–2020) to enterprise AI platform (2020–present) to AI security and governance platform (2026, via Armis) represents textbook existential flexibility — each transformation built on the previous while expanding TAM 10x. The company didn't defend its ITSM monopoly; it redefined the category. The $7.75B Armis acquisition tripling the security TAM is the latest act of existential flexibility[3]. Score: 2 points (transformative flexibility).

courage_to_lead.analysis: McDermott's $3M personal share purchase during the January 2026 software sector rout — when the stock dropped 10%+ on AI disruption fears — is precisely Sinek's definition of courage: acting in service of the Just Cause at personal financial cost. The $7.75B Armis acquisition, the largest in company history, demonstrates willingness to make bold bets to expand the platform[3]. The decision to proactively build AI Governance features for EU AI Act compliance — investing before regulation requires it — shows courage to lead rather than react[5]. The Rule of 55+ standard (revenue growth + FCF margin must exceed 55) maintains long-term capital discipline. Score: 2 points (pattern of courage).

investment_thesis: ServiceNow is the enterprise AI platform that benefits from — rather than is threatened by — the AI boom. Q1 2026 delivered subscription revenue of $3,671M (+22% YoY), beating guidance, with full-year 2026 subscription revenue guidance raised to $15.7–$15.8B (+21% YoY)[1][2]. Now Assist is tracking toward $1.5B in 2026 ACV (raised from $1B), with customers spending $1M+ growing 130%+ YoY. The $7.75B Armis acquisition (completed May 2026) triples the security TAM and launches the new Autonomous Security & Risk product — integrating Armis and Veza to govern every AI agent, identity, and connected asset[3]. CEO McDermott's 2030 contract extension, 'people before profits' leadership style, and the Board's vision to become 'the Defining AI Enterprise Software Company of the 21st Century' signal maximum long-term conviction[4].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on valuation and execution: ServiceNow trades at ~22x forward earnings and a PEG of 2.8 — pricing in a decade of flawless execution at current growth rates. The $7.75B Armis acquisition is the largest in company history and introduces integration complexity at a time when the company is also absorbing Moveworks and launching new AI Governance features[3][5]. If agentic AI matures faster than McDermott's 'execution layer' thesis anticipates, enterprise workflows could be replaced rather than orchestrated by AI systems. Additionally, the consumption-based pricing model that McDermott has advocated — moving from seat-based to value-based pricing — could create revenue volatility if enterprise AI adoption proves lumpy rather than linear. The stock has recovered from the January 2026 sector selloff but remains vulnerable to any growth deceleration given its premium multiple.
key_catalysts: Now Assist reaching $1.5B+ ACV in 2026 — with the target already raised from $1B and customers spending $1M+ growing 130%+ YoY, exceeding this milestone would validate ServiceNow as the enterprise AI platform standard and could trigger multiple expansion[2]

Armis and Autonomous Security & Risk generating incremental revenue and cross-sell opportunities — the $7.75B acquisition tripling the security TAM could accelerate total revenue growth above the current 21% trajectory as security becomes a core platform pillar[3]

AI Governance features becoming the de facto EU AI Act compliance layer — ServiceNow's proactive launch of governance tools within the Now Platform positions it to capture regulatory-driven demand as EU enforcement intensifies in 2026–2027[5]
key_risks: Agentic AI systems maturing to replace enterprise workflow orchestration platforms within 2–3 years — if AI agents can execute end-to-end without ServiceNow's orchestration layer, the 'Operating System for AI' thesis unravels

Premium valuation (~22x forward P/E, PEG 2.8) leaving no margin of safety — any single quarter miss after the January 2026 selloff would be punishing; the $7.75B Armis integration adds execution complexity at a critical moment[3]

Consumption-based pricing transition creating revenue volatility — McDermott's push toward value-based and per-interaction pricing could produce lumpy revenue patterns that spook investors accustomed to predictable subscription growth
